topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The acceptance letter lay crumpled on the worn wooden table, the embossed seal mocking my hopeful expression. I reread the line, the single word ‘Waitlist,’ over and over. My hands began to tremble, a fine, almost imperceptible vibration that made the ink on the page swim. Every muscle in my jaw tightened until it ached. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair backward with a jarring scrape. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the quiet room. I paced the length of the small apartment three times, my breath catching in shallow, rapid gasps. I needed to call the coach, to demand an explanation, but my throat felt constricted, refusing to form the necessary words. My gaze darted to the window, focusing intensely on the distant streetlights as if the answer to my entire future was hidden in their glow.

[story 2]

The fluorescent lights of the gymnasium seemed too bright, exposing every flaw in my performance. I stared at the roster posted on the bulletin board, my vision tunneling until the edges of the room blurred into streaks of grey. My heart hammered against my ribs with a frantic, uneven rhythm, a desperate drumbeat demanding release. I clenched my fists so tightly my knuckles turned white, the pressure building behind my eyes until a dull ache radiated outward. "This can't be happening," I muttered, the words barely audible above the echoing squeak of sneakers from another practice. I started pacing in tight, jerky circles, my movements becoming agitated, unable to settle into any rhythm. I kept running a hand over my damp forehead, trying to cool the sudden heat rising in my cheeks.

[story 3]

The silence in the waiting room was suffocating, thick with unspoken disappointment. I kept picking at a loose thread on my jacket sleeve, the repetitive motion a futile attempt to anchor myself to reality. My stomach churned with a cold, nauseating twist, and I had to press the heels of my hands against my eyes to keep the images—the team photo, the shared locker room smiles—from flooding my vision. Every minute stretched into an eternity, each tick of the clock amplifying the dreadful certainty settling in my chest. I leaned forward, resting my forehead on the cool surface of the table, trying to draw a steadying breath, but it wouldn't come. My shoulders began to shake involuntarily, a deep, uncontrolled tremor running through my frame.
